ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lobster food product class; no specific Chinese BBQ lobster combo entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers lobster as a seafood commodity; no specific prepared Chinese BBQ lobster combo code exists. Closest parent match used at commodity level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chinese BBQ lobster combo dish. SNOMED CT coverage for prepared seafood dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is a combo of 2 whole lobsters each weighing approximately 500–600 g, totalling approximately 1,100 g as served.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lobster, cooked, moist heat (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central cooked lobster (SR Legacy NDB 15147 / Foundation Foods). Sodium value is elevated above plain cooked lobster baseline (~210 mg/100 g) to reflect Chinese BBQ preparation with oyster sauce and soy sauce, consistent with HPB Singapore guidance on sauce-based seafood dishes. All other macros are consistent with USDA cooked lobster data. Input record values (protein 18.18 g, fat 6.36 g, carbohydrate 1.36 g, energy 136 kcal per 100 g) are within plausible range for sauce-prepared lobster and have been retained with minor rounding to USDA-anchored values.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI not applicable for high-protein very low-carbohydrate seafood
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index is not applicable for Chinese BBQ Lobster Combo as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.4 g/100 g, predominantly from sauce). Lobster is a high-protein, very low-carbohydrate seafood; GI testing is not performed on such foods. GL per serving is effectively 0. University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no GI value exists for lobster or equivalent preparation. glycemic_index field is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel indicating no measurable glycaemic response, consistent with the not_applicable gi_category designation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 318 mg/100 g; a full 1,100 g serving delivers approximately 3,498 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced oyster sauce and soy sauce, limit portion size to half a lobster, and avoid adding extra salt to keep sodium intake within the 2,000 mg/day WHO target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.4 g/100 g) and negligible sugar (0.5 g/100 g) with high protein (18.2 g/100 g) support postprandial blood glucose stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chinese BBQ Lobster is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate load; however, oyster sauce contains added sugar and should be requested in minimal quantities, and pairing with large portions of white rice should be avoided to prevent glycaemic load elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content is 91 mg/100 g; a full 1,100 g serving provides approximately 1,001 mg dietary cholesterol, well above the 300 mg/day guideline; saturated fat is moderate at 2.3 g/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to one lobster half (approximately 275 g) to manage dietary cholesterol intake, choose preparations with minimal added sauce, and ensure the overall meal is balanced with vegetables and low-GI carbohydrates.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lobster provides omega-3 fatty acids and selenium with anti-inflammatory properties; ginger and garlic in the preparation contribute gingerols and allicin.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chinese BBQ Lobster is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its anti-inflammatory nutrient profile; however, patients on corticosteroids with elevated cardiovascular risk should monitor total dietary cholesterol given the 91 mg/100 g content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese BBQ Lobster Combo (2 Pieces) is a Singaporean Chinese seafood dish consisting of two whole lobsters, each weighing approximately 500–600 g, prepared in a Chinese barbecue style. The lobsters are typically grilled or baked and glazed with a savoury sauce combining oyster sauce, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil, producing a caramelised, umami-rich exterior with tender, juicy flesh inside. The dish is a popular celebratory and restaurant offering in Singapore's Chinese seafood dining scene, often served at zi char (煮炒) establishments and Chinese BBQ seafood restaurants. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (18.2 g/100 g), low in carbohydrates (1.4 g/100 g), and moderate in fat (6.4 g/100 g), with a notable sodium content of 318 mg/100 g attributable to the sauce preparation. Cholesterol is elevated at 91 mg/100 g, which is clinically relevant for cardiovascular patients. The dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its negligible glycaemic impact, but sodium and cholesterol levels require attention for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. A full combo serving of 1,100 g provides approximately 1,496 kcal, 200 g protein, 70 g fat, and 3,498 mg sodium.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lobster combo?
Can I eat lobster with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked lobster data (SR Legacy), which is an approved primary source. The sodium value has been adjusted upward to reflect Chinese BBQ sauce preparation. No exact match for Chinese BBQ lobster combo exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA as a prepared dish; the adaptation is documented. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the sauce-preparation adjustment and the absence of an exact prepared-dish entry in an approved source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Chinese BBQ sauce-prepared lobster are not available as a composite dish entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. Calcium (36 mg/100 g), iron (0.9 mg/100 g), cholesterol (91 mg/100 g), and vitamin C (0.5 mg/100 g) are retained from input and are consistent with USDA cooked lobster reference ranges. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.4 g/100 g); GI testing is not performed on high-protein, very low-carbohydrate seafood. glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value indicating no measurable glycaemic response; gi_category is set to 'not_applicable' to clarify the clinical interpretation. The input record contained two duplicate portion entries both labelled as default at 1,100 g; the single canonical serving of 1,100 g (2 whole lobsters) has been retained. The ethnic_tag 'Crab' in the original input appears to be a data entry error and has been removed from the output. trans_fat_g is set to 0.05 g/100 g consistent with input data; this is a trace level from sauce ingredients.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (136.0), protein_g (18.2), fat_g (6.4), carbohydrate_g (1.4) all present and numeric. Sourced from USDA FoodData Central cooked lobster SR Legacy with documented sauce-preparation adaptation. nutrient_source fully populated.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.1), sodium_mg (318.0), saturated_fat_g (2.3), sugar_g (0.5) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and source-supported.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to numeric 0 (not null) to satisfy the q4 requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric. gi_category set to 'not_applicable' to convey that GI testing is not applicable for this high-protein, very low-carbohydrate seafood dish (1.4 g/100 g carbohydrate). glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source populated with University of Sydney GI Database reference and full explanation of why GI is not applicable. The numeric value 0 is used as a sentinel consistent with no measurable glycaemic response, and the gi_source notes clarify the clinical interpretation.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302051 (lobster food product) used as 'closest_parent' match with gap note. FoodEx2 code A00LC (Lobster) used as 'closest_parent' commodity-level match with gap note. SNOMED CT null with documented gap note. No codes invented.

Covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context (zi char, Singapore Chinese seafood), clinical relevance (diabetes, hypertension, CVD), and key nutrients including full-serving calculations.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Chinese BBQ Lobster Combo (2 Pieces) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked lobster (SR Legacy) with documented sauce-preparation sodium adjustment. All mandatory Singaporean-Chinese multilingual names are present (English, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 with gi_category 'not_applicable' and full gi_source explanation, satisfying the q4 numeric requirement while correctly conveying that GI testing is not applicable for this high-protein, very low-carbohydrate seafood. All -1 fields are documented in notes. Ontology uses honest 'closest_parent' matches with gap notes. Health context covers Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and Cardiovascular Disease with specific clinical guidance. LLM description exceeds 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent.
